steps.title: Calculate the confidence interval
steps.content:
Margin of error is ±3% around the point estimate of 30%.

Lower bound: 30% − 3% = 27%
Upper bound: 30% + 3% = 33%

The confidence interval is [27%, 33%]. This range represents where we expect the true population percent to fall.

steps.title: Evaluate each statement against the confidence interval
steps.content:
The confidence interval tells us which population values are plausible (inside the interval) and which are doubtful (outside the interval).
  • Choice A: Misinterprets 3% as a sample misclassification rate. Margin of error measures uncertainty when generalizing to the population, not classification accuracy within the sample.
  • Choice B: Says it is not possible for the true percent to be below 27%. That is too absolute; a confidence interval does not make values outside the interval impossible.
  • Choice C: Claims the true percent is 33%. This confuses the upper boundary with the estimate itself. The true value is likely somewhere in [27%, 33%], not specifically 33%.
  • Choice D: Says it is doubtful that the true percent is 35%. Since 35% > 33%, 35% lies outside the confidence interval [27%, 33%]. The word doubtful correctly reflects that values outside the interval are less likely. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
